coredata.dc:title: Oxidized nanocellulose facilitates preparing photoluminescent nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for Fe3+ ions detection and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhenzhen
Chen, Mingjie
Guo, Yanzhu
Zhou, Jinghui
Shi, Qingshan
Sun, Runcang
coredata.dc:description: A major challenge in preparing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from the world’s most abundant material, cellulose, is its limited chemical accessibility, which results in low yield and low quantum yield (QY) of the obtained N-CDs. Herein, we showed that the overall yield and QY of N-CDs from dissolving pulp were significantly improved from 2.9% and 9.7% to 16.1% and 30.3% with an oxidation treatment. The efficient synthesis of N-CDs from dissolving pulp was attributed to the enhanced chemical accessibility of cellulose by the oxidation process. The obtained N-CDs showed a graphite-like structure rich in carboxyl and amino functional groups. They were well monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 1.86 nm. Also, the as-prepared N-CDs showed excellent selective and sensitive response to Fe3+ ions with the detection limit of 1.14 μM. In addition, vitro cytotoxicity test indicated the non-toxicity of N-CDs which enabled their success as fluorescent probe for bio-imaging of Vero cells. Summarily, this study presented an improved strategy to synthesize advanced materials with potential application in sensor, bioimaging and biomedical from the world’s most abundant, cheapest, green and renewable materials-cellulose.

head.ce:title: Oxidized nanocellulose facilitates preparing photoluminescent nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for Fe3+ ions detection and bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A major challenge in preparing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from the world’s most abundant material, cellulose, is its limited chemical accessibility, which results in low yield and low quantum yield (QY) of the obtained N-CDs. Herein, we showed that the overall yield and QY of N-CDs from dissolving pulp were significantly improved from 2.9% and 9.7% to 16.1% and 30.3% with an oxidation treatment. The efficient synthesis of N-CDs from dissolving pulp was attributed to the enhanced chemical accessibility of cellulose by the oxidation process. The obtained N-CDs showed a graphite-like structure rich in carboxyl and amino functional groups. They were well monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 1.86 nm. Also, the as-prepared N-CDs showed excellent selective and sensitive response to Fe3+ ions with the detection limit of 1.14 μM. In addition, vitro cytotoxicity test indicated the non-toxicity of N-CDs which enabled their success as fluorescent probe for bio-imaging of Vero cells. Summarily, this study presented an improved strategy to synthesize advanced materials with potential application in sensor, bioimaging and biomedical from the world’s most abundant, cheapest, green and renewable materials-cellulose.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), newcomers of carbon nano-materials family, are fluorescent, zero-dimensional and spherical nanoparticles with diameters smaller than 10 nm [1,2]. They are capturing increasing attentions from both academia and industry since they were discovered occasionally in 2004 [3]. CDs exhibit many attractive merits, including high fluorescence intensity, excellent water dispersibility, tunable surface functionality and high photostability [4,5]. As compared with organic probes and traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), such as CdS/CdSe and ZnSe QDs [6,7], CDs show low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [8]. As a result, CDs have found wide applications in fields of bio-imaging [9], drug delivery [10] and sensors [11,12] in replacing of traditional semiconductor quantum dots of CdSe.

CDs are conventionally synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of a wide range of carbon sources along with/without heteroatoms doping agents [13–16]. Such carbon sources include molecular precursors, i.e. citrates [17], sugars [18,19], polyaniline (PANI) [20], oligomeric polyethylene glycol (PEG); biomass precursors, i.e. watermelon peel [21], lemon peel [22], milk [23], tea [24], castor seed oil [25], almond husk [26], bamboo leaves [27], pomelo peel [28] and waste materials precursors, i.e. carbon paper [29] and bike soot [30]. Regardless of the wide range of carbon sources for CDs, there were nothing unique found for the as-prepared CDs. As concluded by Fernando et al [31], CDs are not “picky” with carbon sources, because the excited state properties of CDs are dictated by defects.

Then, features such as cheap, easy availability, renewable and environmentally friendly, should be guidelines for choosing a carbon source for CDs. Specifically, raw materials of zero carbon dioxide (CO2)/greenhouse gases emissions should be used in responding to climate changes [32]. Fortunately, the world has such raw material on hand. Cellulose, originated from CO2 by photosynthesis in plant, is featured as the most abundant, cheap and clean bio-resources on earth [33,34]. There are several reports using cellulose as carbon sources for synthesis of CDs with fairly low toxicity [35–38].

However, the synthesis of CDs from cellulose suffers from low yield, high residue rate and relatively low quantum yield (QY). For example, Souza et al. [39] prepared CDs with low QY of 3.2% using kraft pulp as precursor, although hazardous acid, concentrated sulfuric acid, was applied. Wei et al. [40] obtained amorphous CDs using waste paper as precursor via sonication assisted hydrothermal treatment, reporting a bit higher QY of 10.8%. It is well known that cellulose is a highly crystalline polymer. Chemical reactions are limited to start at the surface molecule of cellulose crystals, resulting in the low reactivity of cellulose. As a result, the low QY of CDs from cellulose could be attributed to the limited access of cellulose molecules associated with its high crystallinity. On the other hand, it is reported that functional groups, such as carbonyl groups, are very important for the formation of CDs. Generally, precursors with –OH groups only would result in CDs with QY of <10% [16]. Cellulose is a linear polymer of β (1 → 4) linked d-glucose unit, there are only –OH groups in cellulose molecule without other functional groups. Thus, it seems reasonable that the QY of CDs using cellulose as precursor is low.

However, it is mandatory to improve the QY of CDs from cellulose to make use of this renewable material. Herein, we proposed oxidized nanocellulose as CDs precursor to improve the QY of CDs from dissolving pulp. Such oxidized nanocellulose was produced from softwood dissolving pulp by HNO3 treatment. The HNO3 treatment could simultaneously produce carbonyl groups, aldehyde and carboxylate groups, in cellulose molecule and break down the cellulose crystal into nanoparticle, increasing the accessible surface of cellulose crystals. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with high QY were synthesized with such oxidized nanocellulose. Chemical structures and properties of the as-prepared N-CDs were extensively characterized using various methodologies. The as-prepared N-CDs were water-soluble, and nontoxic spherical particles with an average diameter of 1.86 ± 0.036 nm, suitable for a wide range of applications, such as the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+, cell labeling, and cellular imaging.

Softwood dissolving pulp with an average degree of polymerization (DP) of 884, was used as the starting cellulose source, and supplied by Shandong Bohi Industry Co., Ltd. Rice straw and wheat straw were harvested from local farms (Dalian, China). Sodium nitrite (99% purity), quinine sulfate dehydrates (99% purity) and ammonium ceric nitrate (H8CeN8O18, 99% purity) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). HNO3 (65% w/v), H3PO4 (85% w/v), ethanol (95%), acetone, ammonia solution (28%) and calcium acetate monohydrate (98% purity) were obtained from Kermel Analytical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Chitosan (MW = 100000) and hydroxylammonium chloride were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, BaCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, AlCl3, ZnCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, AgNO3, CrCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). HgCl2 was obtained from Xiya Reagent (Shandong, China). The dialysis membrane (MWCO 500 Da) was obtained from Spectrumlabs (America). All other chemicals were of analytical-reagent grades and used without further purification.

Firstly, 10 g softwood dissolving pulp was completely soaked in 140 mL of HNO3/H3PO4 (2/1 v/v) solution for 0.5 h at room temperature. Desired amounts of NaNO2 (HNO3/H3PO4/ NaNO2 2/1/1.4 v/v/wt%) was slowly added into the above mediums under vigorous mechanical stirring. The oxidation of cellulose was carried out at 65 °C for 24 h by mechanical stirring. After terminating with an excess of deionized water (three times the volume of the reaction mixture), the white precipitates were obtained by centrifugation and washed with ethanol/water solution (2/1 v/v) until the filtrate was neutral. After washed with acetone, the precipitates were finally dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 24 h to obtain OC. The oxidized rice straw, wheat straw and chitosan were obtained using the same oxidation procedure, and named as oxidized rice straw (ORS), oxidized wheat straw (OWS) and oxidized chitosan (OCH), respectively.

The carboxyl content of OC was determined by alkali neutralization titration and calculated according to Eq. (1):

where, C is the concentration of NaOH (mol/L); V and V 0 are the amount of NaOH (mL) consumed by the OC nanoparticle dispersion and blank solution; W is the weight of oven-dried OC sample (g).

The aldehyde group content of OC was determined according to the reported reference [41]. Based on oxime reaction of aldehyde group with hydroxylamine hydrochloride, the released hydrochloric acid was titrated with standard NaOH solution, and recording the consumption of NaOH solution. The aldehyde group content of OC was calculated based on Eq. (2).
(2)

where, C 1 is the NaOH concentration (mol/L); V 2 and V 1 are the amount of NaOH (mL) consumed by the OC nanoparticle dispersion and blank solution; M is the weight of oven-dried OC sample (g).

Briefly, 0.5 g OC was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water, followed by sonicated for 1 h with a power of 75 W and every 2 s working time for 3 s break time to obtain the well-dispersed OC nanoparticle solution. Desired amounts of ammonia were slowly added into the solution. After that, the mixtures were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and the autoclave was placed in a pre-heated oil bath at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown suspension was further ultrasonic treated for 30 min, and then centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min twice to obtain the supernatant. The resultant supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm filter, and dialyzed against deionized water in dialysis membrane (MWCO 500 Da) for 48 h. After freeze-dried for 72 h, the purified N-CDs powders from OC were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yield and QY of fluorescent N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The ultrapure water was added into purified N-CDs concentrated solution until the volume of N-CDs solution was 50 mL. 5 mL prepared N-CDs solution was dried in an oven at 107 °C for 40 h and the accurate dried weight of N-CDs was obtained. Yield of N-CDs was calculated using the following Eq. (3).
(3)
Yield
%
=
M 2
-
M 1
× 10 / 0.5 × 100 %
where M 1 is the quality of weighing bottle, M 2 is the quality of weighing bottle containing dry N-CDs, 0.5 g is the quality of OC and 10 is the multiple of volume of as-prepared N-CDs solution to the volume of N-CDs solution for weight determination.

Quinine sulphate with a 54% QY in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the standard to calculate the QY of N-CDs [42]. The UV absorbance values of all the N-CDs samples were adjusted to 0.05–0.1 to minimize the inner filter effect. The QY of fluorescent N-CDs was calculated using the following Eq. (4).

where the subscripts ST and X refer to the standard reference and sample solutions, respectively, Φ represents the fluorescent quantum yield, I is the measured integrated fluorescent intensity, A represents the absorbance value, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of cellulose, OC and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of cellulose samples were obtained by a microscope (JEM-7800F, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 12.5 V. The morphologies of OC and N-CDs samples were characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a 6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with the Cu Kα radiation source (40 kV, 30 mA) in the range of 10° to 60°, and all samples were scanned at a speed of 4°/min at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of all samples were obtained on a Fourier spectrometer (Frontier I, PerkinElmer, America). The Raman spectra were collected on a LabRAM ARAMIS Raman spectroscope equipped with a 785 nm laser source (HORIBA Scientific Jobin, France). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out on an ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) surface analysis system. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra were acquired a UV1006M031 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Inc., China). The fluorescent emission spectra were recorded using F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a voltage of 400 V. The slit widths of excitation and emission are 10 nm and 5 nm, respectively. The atomic-force microscopy (AFM) image was performed on a Nanoscope V Multimode 8 scanning probe microscope (Bruker Corporation, America). The experiment was carried out with the AFM probe under ambient conditions (temperature of 25 °C, relative humidity of 25%). The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs and Fe3+ coordinated N-CDs aqueous solution were recorded on a FLS980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The zeta potential of N-CDs aqueous solution was measured on a Nano-ZS ZEN90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) at 25 °C.

The N-CDs solution with an actual concentration of 0.02 mg/mL at pH of 3 was initially prepared. Various metal ion salts solutions with a 2000 μM concentration, including MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, BaCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CaCl2, AlCl3, ZnCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, AgNO3, CrCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, HgCl2 and H8CeN8O18, were prepared and adjusted to pH of 3 using 0.1 M NaOH or HCl solution. After addition of 2 mL metal ions solutions into 2 mL N-CDs solution and incubation for 3 min, the fluorescent intensity was measured using an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and emission wavelength of 410 nm, which was defined as F. The fluorescent intensity of 0.01 mg/mL N-CDs solution was defined as F0.

To evaluate the sensitivity towards Fe3+ and Ce4+, 2 mL of 0.02 mg/mL N-CDs solution was added to a quartz cuvette. Two milliliter different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (pH = 3) were added into the above quartz cuvette. After 3 min, all of the fluorescent emission spectra were recorded. The LOD (limit of detection) was calculated based on the following Eq. (5)
(5)
LOD = 3 σ / K
where K is the slope for the range of the linearity, σ is the standard deviation of the blank (n = 11).

The cytotoxicity of N-CDs was evaluated by the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Firstly, Vero cells (1 × 104 cells per well) in 100 μL Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) solution were seeded in each well of a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C in the incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h. After incubation, the DMEM culture medium was replaced by the N-CDs with concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL and further incubated for 24 h or 48 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Each sample was performed in triplicate. The DMEM culture medium was carefully removed, and then 100 μL CCK-8 solution was added into each well and continued incubated for 4 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Finally, the absorbance of each well was recorded at 450 nm using Thermo Multiskan Go micro-plate reader (Thermo, America). Vero cells without added N-CDs were used as a control under the same incubation conditions. Cell viability was calculated by the percentage of absorbance of the Vero cells incubated with N-CDs to that of the control samples without N-CDs.

Vero cells (1–1.5 × 104 cells per mL) were seeded to the confocal petri dish and incubated in DMEM medium for 24 h at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere in the incubator. For cell imaging containing N-CDs, 50 μL N-CDs solution was filtered through at 0.22 μm filter and then mixed with 450 μL the DMEM medium. The mixtures were added above the confocal petri dish containing Vero cells, and further cultured for 4 h at 37 °C. After removing the DMEM medium, the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4), and tracked via a Zeiss LSM 700 laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 488 nm, Zeiss, Germany). In the following, to confirm the fluorescent quenching effect of Fe3+ ions on N-CDs in cells, the cells were incubated with the DMEM medium containing N-CDs-Fe3+ for a further 30 min at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Finally, the cells were washed carefully three times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) for observation by confocal imaging.

OC, rich in carboxyl and aldehyde groups, was prepared by oxidation of softwood dissolving pulp with HNO3/H3PO4/NaNO2 oxidants (Fig. 1 a). The softwood dissolving pulp was fibrous material with diameter of 28 μm and length of >600 μm by SEM (Fig. 1b and 1c). It showed a typical XRD pattern of cellulose Ⅰ (Fig. 1d) with diffraction peaks at 2θ = 15.0° (ī10), 16.8° (1 1 0), 20.5° (0 1 2), 22.7° (2 0 0) and 34.5° (0 0 4), crystalline index (CrI) of 79%, and crystal size of 5.2 nm [43,44]. The fibric structure of dissolving pulp associated with high crystalline index made it intact upon hydrothermal treatment [45,46]. To increase the reactivity of dissolving pulp upon hydrothermal treatment, carboxyl and aldehyde groups were introduced to cellulose molecules through selective oxidation with HNO3/H3PO4/NaNO2 oxidants (Fig. 1a). The oxidized pulp turned out to be spherical nanoparticles with diameter of 19 nm (Fig. 1e and 1f). Such OC nanoparticles were well dispersed in water forming semitransparent gel-like solution (Fig. 1a) because of the introduction of carboxyl and aldehyde groups. The carboxyl and aldehyde groups content of OC were calculated to be 3.79 mmol/g and 1.7 mmol/g by titration, respectively, which were much higher than those of OC prepared from cellulose by previous reports [47,48].

The introduction of carboxyl and aldehyde groups to OC were confirmed by FT-IR studies (Fig. 1g). New peaks were observed from the OC sample at 1733 and 1292 cm−1 which are assigned to CO stretching vibration of carboxyl and aldehyde groups and C-O vibration of carboxyl groups, respectively [49,50]. The peaks at 897 and 815 cm−1 are assigned to C1-H deformation of β-1,4 glucosic linkage and C–H deformation of glucose rings of dissolving pulp, respectively [49]. These peaks shifted to 919 and 858 cm−1, respectively, because of the introduction of carboxyl and aldehyde groups. The peaks at 1337 and 1318 cm−1 are assigned to CH2 rocking vibration and wagging at C6 of anhydroglucose unit (AGU) of dissolving pulp [49,51]. The intensity of those peaks decreased after the oxidation of dissolving pulp, indicating the oxidation of the C6 position of AGU. The peak at 710 cm−1 is assigned to cellulose Ⅰβ polymorph. This peak remained intact upon the oxidation of dissolving pulp, indicating that the oxidation did not modify cellulose Iβ polymorph.

The intact cellulose crystalline polymorph was further confirmed by XRD studies (Fig. 1d). The OC sample showed typical XRD pattern of cellulose Ⅰ with diffraction peaks at 2θ = 15.0° (ī10), 16.8° (1 1 0), 20.5° (0 1 2), 22.7° (2 0 0) and 34.5° (0 0 4), CrI of 47%, and crystal size of 4.2 nm. This result indicated that the oxidation process did not modify the cellulose Ⅰ crystalline polymorph but decreased the crystalline index and crystal’s size of cellulose [52]. The introduction of carboxyl and aldehyde groups and the decrease of crystalline index and crystal’s size of cellulose increased the accessibility of cellulose as carbon source for CDs synthesis.

Importantly, the QY of the as-prepared N-CDs from OC was 30.3%, which was much higher than that of N-CDs from dissolving pulp (9.7%) and those from cellulose (below 13.5%) by previous studies [37,39,53–56]. The reason why N-CDs from OC showed a higher QY than that of N-CDs from the dissolving pulp was probably due to the present of carboxylate and aldehyde groups in the OC sample. However, the photoluminescence mechanism of N-CDs is not clear yet and various explanations were reported by different studies [57]. Overall, increasing the accessibility of cellulose will favor improving the QY of obtained N-CDs.

Besides, various biomass materials, which were composed by cellulose, were applied as carbon sources for N-CDs synthesis with the present methodology (Table S1). The results showed that the oxidation treatment was favorable for the preparation of N-CDs with high yield and QY. In addition, chitosan, another natural polymer with high crystallinity and structure analog to cellulose, was pretreated with the present oxidation method and subsequently used as carbon resource for N-CDs synthesis. The yield and QY of N-CDs from chitosan were increased from 4.7% and 10.5% to 23.9% and 16.6%, respectively. These results suggested that the oxidation treatment was suitable for the synthesis of N-CDs with high yield and QY from various biomass materials, which were composed by cellulose or other polysaccharides similar to cellulose.

Another important issue is the scaling-up synthesis of N-CDs. In this case, 0.22 g of N-CDs with a yield of 44.0 wt% was prepared from 0.5-gram OC. Such a scale was comparable to some of the reported gram-scale synthesis of CDs in view of the yield of CDs, i.e. Ding et al. [58] reported a 3-gram synthesis of CDs from lignin with a yield 21% (0.61 g CDs). Though there were some reports addressing tens-gram scale synthesis of CDs [42,59–61], none of these reports addressed the issue caused by the scaling-up itself, like mass and heat transfer issues, energy efficiency, optimization of the washing process, waste management and effects of scaling-up and washing process on the properties of resultant CDs. Such issues should be addressed in an effort on pursuing a large-scale production of CDs.

However, the scaling up itself would be another story as there would be some other problems to be solved as is mentioned above. The cost of the process should be evaluated on an optimized condition. When doing a techno-economic analysis, cost of energy consumption, labor, waste management, solvent recycle and environment impact besides the chemicals themselves should be taken into account. On the other hand, the washing process that was applied by this study is not desired for a techno-economic analysis, but desired for a mechanism study. However, the washing process consume huge amount of solvent which will count a lot when doing a techno-economic analysis. Taken all these together, we decided not to make a techno-economic discussion on the scaling-up of the process, but just mention the issues that should be addressed upon the scaling-up of the process, to make our story strong, clear and focused.

The structure of the obtained N-CDs with a 30.3% QY, from chemical to morphology, was comprehensively studied by XPS, FTIR, Raman spectrum, XRD and TEM methodologies. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the XPS survey spectra of OC and CDs displayed C1s and O1s peaks at 284.1 eV and 532.1 eV, respectively, while that of N-CDs showed three peaks at 284.3 eV, 399.0 eV and 531.1 eV, relating to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. These results demonstrated that nitrogen element had been doped into the N-CDs [62]. The C/O ratio of OC was 1.57. It was significantly increased to 2.55 and 4.26 for CDs and N-CDs, respectively. These results indicated the dehydration and degradation of OC by hydrothermal treatment.

The specific elemental composition of N-CDs was C (72.54%), N (10.44%) and O (17.03%). As shown in Fig. 2b, the N1s high-resolution spectra of N-CDs exhibited three peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.6 eV and 400.8 eV, which are attributed to the –N, C–N and N–H bonds, respectively. These results further confirmed that nitrogen element had been doped into the N-CDs [63,64]. The C1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2c) of OC (bottom) and CDs (middle) exhibited three peaks at 284.5/284.5 eV, 286.4/286.2 eV and 287.8/288.5 eV, which are assigned to C–C, C–O and CO bonds, respectively. The high resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2c) of N-CDs (upper) were deconvoluted into four peaks at C–C (284.6 eV), C-N (285.4 eV), C-O (286.2 eV) and CO (287.5 eV) bonds. The O1s high-resolution spectra of OC (bottom), CDs (middle) and N-CDs (upper) are shown in Fig. 2d. The peaks at 531.0/531.0/531.0 eV, 532.0/531.8/532.0 eV and 532.8/532.7/532.8 eV could be clearly observed in O1s spectra of the OC, CDs and N-CDs samples, which are attributed to CO, C–O–C and C–OH groups, respectively [65].

The presence of functional groups in the N-CDs samples were confirmed by FTIR (Fig. 3 a). The peaks at 3154 cm−1, 1600 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 assigned to N–H of amine groups and O–H stretching vibrations, CO/C-N stretching vibrations and N–H bending vibration, respectively [66,67]. The presence of these peaks in the N-CDs samples confirmed the doping of nitrogen element into the N-CDs via chemical reaction. The peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 950 cm−1 are assigned to O–H and C–O stretching vibration, respectively, demonstrating the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the N-CDs samples. These oxygen-containing and amino functional groups enhanced the stability and dispersibility of N-CDs in aqueous system. The intensity of the peaks assigned CO and C–O stretching vibrations of the CDs samples were much weaker than that of the N-CD samples, indicating the high dehydration of OC in the presence of nitrogen passivator.

The as-prepared CDs and N-CDs were proved to display a graphite-like structure by Raman spectra (Fig. 3b and 3c). As shown in Fig. 3b, the D peak at 1357 cm−1 is corresponded to the vibrations of the disordered sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [68]. The G peak at 1568 cm−1 is associated with the vibrations of the ordered sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [11]. The ID/IG ratio (calculated by the area of D peak to G peak) of CDs was 3.24, suggesting that there was an abundant of structural defects in CDs. The D and G peaks of N-CDs were observed at 1331 and 1558 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 3c). The ID/IG value of N-CDs was found to be decreased to 1.33. This result indicated that N-CDs had fewer defects than CDs and thus showed higher QY, and possessed graphite-like structure of the resultant N-CDs [58,69]. The graphite structure of N-CDs was further confirmed by XRD studies (Fig. 3d). The CDs showed a broad diffraction peak at about 23°. The peak of N-CDs shifted to 24° (d002 = 0.82 nm), indicating the presence of graphite crystalline structure (0 0 2) [18].

The morphology of the N-CDs was characterized by TEM and AFM. The results in Fig. 3e showed that they were well-dispersed in water and possessed of spherical morphologies. The particle diameter of N-CDs was distributed in the range of 1–3.4 nm (Fig. 3e), which was similar to those of previous reports [70,71]. Furthermore, the average particle diameter of N-CDs was 1.86 ± 0.036 nm (based on statistical analysis of more than 100 dots using Nano Measure software) and the particle size distribution was consistent with the Gaussian distribution. The AFM image revealed the topographic morphology of the N-CDs (Fig. 3f). Fig. 3f inset displayed an AFM trace between three particles. The heights were in the range of 1 to 4 nm with an average thickness of 2 nm, corresponding to 1–4 graphene layers [58,69,72]. The zeta potential analysis was carried out to estimate the electronic charge on the surface of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1a, the zeta potential value of N-CDs was −11.5 mV, suggesting that the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxylic groups, on the surface of the as-prepared N-CDs [73,74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra were applied to understand the optical properties of CDs and N-CDs aqueous solutions. The CDs and N-CDs aqueous solutions were yellowish, clear and transparent under daylight. Both of them emitted blue fluorescence under UV lamp (365 nm). The blue fluorescent emission ability of N-CDs was much stronger than that of CDs (Fig. 4 a top-right inset). The aqueous solution of CDs had a weak absorption peak at about 240 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. The N-CDs solution showed two peaks at around 273 nm and 310 nm, assigned to n-π* transition of C-N and n-π* of CO transition, respectively. The peak at 310 nm is originated from the trapping of excited energy of the surface states, which would result in stronger blue fluorescence [75,76]. In addition, the excitation and emission peaks of the N-CDs aqueous solution were observed at 320 nm and 410 nm, further indicating the fluorescence emission ability of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs showed a typical excitation-dependent feature. The emission peaks of N-CDs were gradually red-shifted when the excitation wavelength was altered from 300 nm to 410 nm. The N-CDs exhibited maximum excitation at 320 nm and maximum emission at 410 nm.

In order to guide the application of N-CDs for metal ion sensing and biological imaging, pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4c, the optimal pH to obtain the strongest fluorescence intensity was pH = 5. Importantly, the fluorescence emission peak of N-CDs was gradually blue-shifted along with the increasing pH value from 1 to 13. These results suggested that the carboxyl groups of N-CDs could lead to fluorescence quenching in strong acid and alkali environment. In addition, ionic effects on the fluorescence properties of N-CDs aqueous solution were studied to guide the application of N-CDs in more complex environment. The influence of sodium chloride ionic concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is shown in Fig. 4d. Negligible changes of the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs were found upon increasing the sodium chloride ionic concentrations from 10−4 M to 1.0 M. This result suggested that the obtained N-CDs could work under high ionic strength conditions. In comparison with organic solvents, the as-prepared N-CDs exhibited stronger fluorescence emission in water (Fig. 4e). The effect of irradiation time on the fluorescence stability of N-CDs was carried out under UV light irradiation (365 nm) within 180 min. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs aqueous solution was measured every 20 min interval. As shown in Fig. 4f, the relative fluorescence intensity of N-CDs aqueous solution was about 95% even after 180 min of irradiation under UV light. Overall, the change of the fluorescence intensity was insignificant. This result might be due to the rich carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs, which made N-CDs possess the stable fluorescence property against photobleaching. Such N-CDs aqueous solution was beneficial for metal ions sensing and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging with N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Iron is one of the most common metallic elements in the environment and plays an important role in biological systems. In addition, iron is essential for living cells and organisms by participating in various metabolic processes, such as electron transfer, oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis, and RNA and DNA synthesis. Therefore, the detection of iron ion is of great significance because of metal ion pollution in daily wastewater. In this work, we successfully prepared N-CDs with excellent fluorescence performance using one-step hydrothermal method involving OC and ammonia as precursors. The effect of metal ions on fluorescence quenching of as-prepared N-CDs was further evaluated. As depicted in Scheme 2 a, the N-CDs emitted blue fluorescence under UV radiation. Upon the addition of Fe3+ ions to the N-CDs solution, N-CDs exhibited fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The fluorescence quenching phenomenon was owned to electron transfer between Fe3+ ions and nitrogen-rich and oxygen-rich groups of N-CDs [77,78]. In comparison with Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ cations, Fe3+ ions showed stronger binding affinity towards the electron-rich groups. Thus, it was able to combine with carboxyl and amino electron-donating groups of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1b, the surface functional groups of N-CDs were combined with Fe3+ ions in the aqueous solution, which exhibited the positive zeta potential (22.5 mV). To prove this fluorescence mechanism, the electron transfer of functional groups of N-CDs is showed in Scheme 2b. The excited electron of N-CDs would form radiation electron/hole recombination, emitting blue-fluorescence in the absence of Fe3+. However, the addition of Fe3+ ions resulted in the transmission of the excited electrons to the 3d unfilled orbit of Fe3+, which hindered the formation of radiation electron/hole recombination, and then generated fluorescence quenching.

To further understand the Fe3+ sensing performance of N-CDs, the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ ions was studied at weak-acidic condition (pH = 3). Fig. 5 a showed the time-dependence of fluorescence of N-CDs solution in the presence of 200 μM Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased in 3 min, and no change was observed from 3 min to 5 min. The selectivity of the Fe3+ sensing was found by a control experiment with various metal ions sensing. Fig. 5b showed the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the presence of various metal ions at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. It was found that Fe3+ and Ce4+ ions both displayed the severe fluorescence quenching capabilities of the metal ions sensing. The fluorescence quenching value of N-CDs towards Fe3+ and Ce4+ ions was 98%, 94%, respectively, significantly higher than that of any others reported [78].

The sensitivity of Fe3+ ions sensing of the N-CDs was further studied. The fluorescence emission intensity of the N-CDs was measured in 3 min in the presence of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence emission intensity at 410 nm was almost completely quenched at 1000 μM concentration of Fe3+. Hence, N-CDs could be used as sensing probes for the fluorescence turn-off detection of Fe3+. Fig. 5d showed the relationship between F0/F value and the concentration of Fe3+ in range of 0–1000 μM. In Fe3+ concentration range of 0 to 100 μM, it fitted well with the linear Stern-Volmer equation with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.992, indicating a good linearity between F0/F value and the concentration of Fe3+. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant (K SV) was determined to be 6680 M−1, indicating the high fluorescence quenching efficiency of N-CDs by Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.14 μM based on the three times the standard deviation rule.

As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Ce4+ ions. The Stern-Volmer plots of F0/F showed excellent linearity with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.992 with a LOD calculated as 1.51 μM. Since the strongest fluorescence quencher (Ce4+) was unlikely environmental metal ion pollution in daily wastewater, we focused the attention on the sensing of Fe3+ ions. The as-prepared N-CDs from OC for detection Fe3+ was comparable to other reported carbon dots prepared from other precursors, which are shown in Table 2 . The as-prepared N-CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ ions. When compared with other reported carbon dots for detection of Fe3+ ions, the presented N-CDs from OC possessed higher QY (30.3%), wider linear range (0–100 μM), higher K SV (6680 M−1) and relatively low LOD (1.14 μM) of Fe3+ ions. The results indicated that the as-prepared N-CDs were close or better than the sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching response in the previous literatures reported [79,80]. Moreover, our approach for preparing N-CDs from OC was green, facile, and economic and utilized the abundant and renewable material, dissolving pulp as a starting precursor.

Usually, the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching process could be classified as dynamic and static quenching by lifetime measurement technique. Static quenching occurred in the formation of a complex between the ground state of fluorescence groups and quencher. However, dynamic quenching referred as a result of the collision of excited state of fluorophore and quencher. The analysis of fluorescence lifetime was an effective and definitive way to distinguish between static and dynamic quenching in previous reports [89]. Thus, to better study the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by introducing Fe3+, the time resolved fluorescence-decay curves of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions were tested and shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was about 11.23 ns, which shifted to 10.64 ns after introducing Fe3+ ions. This result indicated that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs towards Fe3+ was a dynamic process [90].

To be applied in field of bioimaging, drug delivery and disease diagnosis, non-toxicity of the N-CDs were essential. The cytotoxicity of as-prepared N-CDs using the standard CCK-8 cell was assayed according to corresponding references [91,92]. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the obtained N-CDs were non-toxic to the Vero at concentration of 200 μg/mL after 24 h incubation, and the cell viabilities were close to 90% at concentration of 50 μg/mL after 48 h incubation, while it remained over 75% at high concentration (200 μg/mL) after 48 h incubation. The results indicated that the as-prepared N-CDs were non-toxicity and could be used as fluorescent probe in cellular imaging. On the basis of their excellent fluorescence properties and low cytotoxicity, the application of N-CDs in cellular imaging was evaluated by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Fig. 7b-g). Vero cells were incubated with 200 μg/mL N-CDs solution for 4 h, and then subjected to confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis. The Vero cells treated with N-CDs exhibited no observed changes and damages in shape. A strong blue-colored fluorescence on the cytoplasmic area of Vero cells suggested that the N-CDs were able to penetrate into the cells after 4 h incubation and maintained their fluorescence properties within the cells (Fig. 7a-c). No fluorescence could be observed from Vero cells incubated presence of both N-CDs and Fe3+ (Fig. 7e-g). All these results suggested that N-CDs could be used as a potential fluorescent agent for cell labeling and cellular imaging with strong fluorescence and low toxicity.

In summary, a strategy of oxidation pretreatment of dissolving pulp was proposed to achieve at N-CDs with high yield and QY. The yield and QY of N-CDs were improved from 2.9% and 9.7% to 16.1% and 30.3% with the prior to HNO3/H3PO4/NaNO2 oxidation treatment. The success in synthesis of N-CDs with high yield and QY was due to the improved accessibility of cellulose molecules associated with the introduction of various functional groups and the de-crystallinity of cellulose. The as-prepared spherical N-CDs showed an average particle diameter of 1.86 ± 0.036 nm with good water dispersibility. It was showed to be an effective fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ with LOD as low as 1.14 μM, with stable fluorescence property against ionic strength and low cytotoxicity, which was a type of potential fluorescent agent for cell labeling and cellular imaging. This strategy offered an improved method for developing N-CDs from cheap, abundant, renewable and environmentally friendly lignocellulosic biomass with high yield and high efficiency, which was suitable for sensitive fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing, bioimaging and biomedical applications.
